text: Using PTR-TOF-MS to Analyze High Resolution Biomass Burning Emissions (FB) During the 4th Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4) From April 24 through May 18, 2012, a high resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) was operated to measure detailed hourly biomass burning emissions of various fuels from fires burning peat, crop residue, and cooking fires, and many other fuels in a controlled fire laboratory setting. The analysis combined calibrations with a gas standard for some for which comprehensive composition had been determined through detailed mass spectrometry studies, along with use of a mass-dependent calibration curve to establish quantitative values for less characterized species in fire emissions. Assignments of likely identities were obtained for many major “exact masses” of high and low molecular weight including numerous high molecular weight values not previously measured from biomass burning. Quantitative values for species identified as having likely values that made up about 80–96% of the total non-methane organic compound (NMOC) detected from major biomass fuels, along with those from FTIR spectroscopy, were combined with laboratory-generated Fire Emission Factors (EFs) for these fuels to generate detailed information concerning detailed biomass-burning emission compounds and EFs for several globally significant biomass fuels. An analysis of traditional cooking and new advance cookstove designs indicates that emission factors are highest for open three-stone cooking relative to new cookstove designs. Measurements revealed that oxygenated compounds comprised the largest fraction of biomass burning emissions in all classes of compounds measured here. Additional analyses concerning furans, aromatic hydrocarbons and phenolics in BB and FB emissions were obtained for the first time. Several nitrogen-containing organic compounds measured from multiple biomass fires have never been measured previously, and comprise a small fraction of the fuel nitrogen contained in each fire but potentially could play roles in the formation of new particles.
explanation: The passage uses highly specific, domain-focused terminology and concrete experimental details that are typical of real scientific reporting. While advanced AI can imitate this, the level of authentic methodology phrasing suggests human authorship.
